target.title: feat(agents): per-agent outbound A2A allowlist override
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `agents.list[].tools.agentToAgent.allow` to control which agents a specific agent may contact via session tools (`sessions_send`, `sessions_list`, `sessions_history`, `session_status`)
- When unset, the agent falls back to the global `tools.agentToAgent.allow` list; when set to explicit IDs, only those targets are reachable; `[]` isolates the agent from all cross-agent access
- The global `tools.agentToAgent.allow` continues to act as a participation gate (both requester and target must match it) before per-agent outbound rules are evaluated
- Outbound-only semantics: only the requester's override is checked, not the target's
- Replace `matchesAllow` with `matchesGlobalParticipation` and add `evaluateAccess()` returning a structured `AgentToAgentDenyReason` so all call sites produce context-aware error messages via `formatAgentToAgentAccessError()`
- Scrub deleted agent references from per-agent outbound allowlists in `pruneAgentConfig()`

### Example config (hub-spoke pattern)

```json
{
  "tools": {
    "agentToAgent": { "enabled": true, "allow": ["*"] }
  },
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      { "id": "david",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["*"] } } },
      { "id": "spoke",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["david"] } } },
      { "id": "kelly",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["david"] } } }
    ]
  }
}
```

spoke→david ✅ | spoke→kelly ❌ | david→anyone ✅

## Test plan

- [x] `sessions-access.test.ts`: per-agent override, deny-all `[]`, global gate, target override ignored (outbound-only), fallback to global when unset
- [x] `sessions.test.ts`: per-agent outbound deny during label lookup in `sessions_send`
- [x] `openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts`: per-agent outbound deny for cross-agent `session_status`
- [x] `agents.test.ts`: `pruneAgentConfig` scrubs deleted agent from per-agent allowlists
- [x] Build passes
- [x] Manual: configure hub-spoke agents (david/spoke/kelly), verify per-agent outbound rules — see `Real behavior proof` section below

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: per-agent outbound `agentToAgent.allow` correctly gates cross-agent session tool access. Hub-spoke topology used: `david` (hub, `allow=["*"]`), `spoke` (allow=`["david"]`), `kelly` (allow=`["david"]`). Validates the new `evaluateAccess()` decision pipeline reaches the documented per-agent outbound semantics, with the structured `per_agent_outbound` deny reason surfaced via `formatAgentToAgentAccessError()`.

- **Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 22.04 (kernel 5.15), Node 22.22.0, pnpm 10. Branch HEAD `55819f6f43` of `repair/pr-39102-a2a-refresh` (this PR rebased on current main). Live invocation of the production export `createAgentToAgentPolicy` from `src/plugin-sdk/session-visibility.ts` — no mocks, no test runner, the same code path that backs `sessions_send`, `sessions_list`, `sessions_history`, and `session_status` at runtime.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

  1. Author a hub-spoke `OpenClawConfig` matching the docs example (david hub + spoke + kelly).
  2. Build `policy = createAgentToAgentPolicy(cfg)`.
  3. Call `policy.evaluateAccess(requester, target)` for the five edge pairs.
  4. Run with: `pnpm tsx proof-hub-spoke.ts` (the script imports the worktree source directly so the running code is the patched export).

- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output, copied verbatim from `node` stdout via `tsx`.

  ```
  # per-agent A2A allowlist — hub-spoke proof
  # config: david=hub (allow *), spoke=spoke (allow david), kelly=spoke (allow david)

  [ok ] spoke -> david: ALLOWED
  [ok ] spoke -> kelly: DENIED (reason=per_agent_outbound)
  [ok ] david -> kelly: ALLOWED
  [ok ] david -> spoke: ALLOWED
  [ok ] kelly -> spoke: DENIED (reason=per_agent_outbound)

  all cases match expected behavior
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: all five real invocations matched the documented hub-spoke semantics. Cross-spoke calls (`spoke -> kelly`, `kelly -> spoke`) were denied with the structured `per_agent_outbound` reason code from `AgentToAgentDecision`. Hub-rooted calls (`david -> spoke`, `david -> kelly`) and spoke-to-hub (`spoke -> david`) were allowed. Process exit code: `0`. Console output above is the literal stdout, no post-processing.

- **What was not tested**: end-to-end IPC roundtrip through the live `sessions_send` agent process pair (single-machine ACP transport). The four session call sites all delegate the access decision to the single `evaluateAccess()` policy proven above, and the wiring of each call site is covered by the unit tests already listed in the Test plan.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: Security: add configurable unpaired DM responses
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: unknown DM senders in pairing mode always received a branded OpenClaw reply with a pairing code.
- Why it matters: operators could not choose a silent or de-branded pairing posture without changing DM policy semantics.
- What changed: added `unpairedResponse` config support (`silent | code-only | branded`) and wired it through the shared pairing helpers and core DM pairing flows.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default behavior remains `branded`, and non-pairing DM policies are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #29945
- Related #29945

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional `unpairedResponse` config for DM pairing flows on core messaging channels.
- `silent` suppresses the automatic reply, `code-only` removes OpenClaw branding, and `branded` preserves current behavior.
- Defaults stay backward compatible: existing installs continue to send the branded pairing reply unless configured otherwise.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp, Discord, Telegram, Slack, Signal, iMessage, LINE, plugin runtime pairing callers
- Relevant config (redacted): default config plus `unpairedResponse` overrides in channel config

### Steps

1. Configure a DM channel with `dmPolicy: pairing`.
2. Send a DM from an unapproved sender.
3. Set `unpairedResponse` to `silent`, `code-only`, and `branded` and compare the emitted reply behavior.

### Expected

- Pairing mode should respect the configured reply style without changing allowlist/pairing authorization behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, pairing replies were always branded and could not be suppressed or de-branded.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: new `buildPairingReply` modes and silent-path behavior in `issuePairingChallenge`; end-to-end build completed after wiring core channel call sites.
- Edge cases checked: silent mode skips outbound reply send; code-only mode still includes the pairing code and approval command; branded mode remains unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live channel delivery against external services.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `unpairedResponse` from config to fall back to the existing branded reply, or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: channel config entries using `unpairedResponse`; pairing helper changes in `src/pairing/*`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: pairing requests created without a reply when `unpairedResponse` is not set, or null reply handling regressions in DM pairing paths.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a DM pairing caller could miss the new nullable reply contract and fail to send or crash.
  - Mitigation: updated the shared helper plus direct call sites, covered the shared helper with tests, and validated with `pnpm build` and `pnpm check`.

target.title: Gateway: allow extension origins in browser allowlist
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` could not explicitly allowlist `chrome-extension://` because the origin checker normalized extension origins through `URL.origin`, which becomes `"null"` for that scheme. - Why it matters: extension sidepanel clients had to rely on `["*"]` to get through browser-origin WS checks, which weakened the intended hardening. - What changed: the origin matcher now keeps exact URL parsing/validation but falls back to exact `scheme://host` matching for non-standard origins, so `chrome-extension://` can be allowlisted exactly; focused unit and handshake tests were added. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no wildcard/prefix expansion, no acceptance of literal `Origin: null`, no changes to token/device auth, host-header fallback, or non-browser client auth behavior. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #35035 (issue mentions app://* origin, but it's the same underlying problem) - Related #35396, #35057 (both PRS were fixes for this problem but were closed because the authors were banned AFAIK) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` can now contain exact extension origins such as `chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop`, and those browser-origin Gateway WS connects will succeed when otherwise authenticated. - Operators no longer need `["*"]` solely to let a Chrome extension sidepanel client connect. - Existing exact matches for normal `http(s)` origins, wildcard behavior, host-header fallback, and literal `Origin: null` rejection remain unchanged. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` (strictly speaking this allows chrome extensions/electron+tauri apps to connect but I think calling it a new capability is a stretch) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `N/A` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local Bun + Vitest - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WS browser-origin client; Chrome extension origin emulated in handshake tests via `ws` - Relevant config (redacted): `gateway.auth.mode="token"`, `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]` ### Steps 1. Configure the Gateway with token auth and `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]`. 2. Open a WS connection that sends `Origin: chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop` and a valid `connect` request. 3. Observe the handshake result. ### Expected - An exactly allowlisted extension origin should be accepted, just like an exactly allowlisted `https://...` origin. ### Actual - Before this fix, the request was rejected with `origin not allowed` because the checker collapsed the extension origin to `"null"` internally. - After this fix, the handshake succeeds and returns `hello-ok`. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: whitelisted the chrome extension I'm developing and successfully connected to the Gateway via WS - Edge cases checked: N/A - What you did **not** verify: did not try with other protocols (`app://`, `tauri://`) ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: `N/A` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit; if extension access is urgently blocked, temporarily fall back to `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["*"]` until the regression is understood. - Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/origin-check.ts`; optionally restore prior `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` values in config. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: an exactly allowlisted `chrome-extension://` still being rejected; malformed/non-matching non-standard origins unexpectedly being accepted; regressions in existing `http(s)` origin allowlisting.

target.title: doctor(config): auto-prune removed google-antigravity-auth entries
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: users can keep stale `plugins.entries["google-antigravity-auth"]` config after the plugin was removed, which keeps emitting warnings (`#27371`).
- Why it matters: users must manually edit config after updates; startup/doctor warnings stay noisy until they do.
- What changed: doctor compatibility normalization now prunes stale removed plugin references from `plugins.entries`, `plugins.installs`, `plugins.allow`, `plugins.load.paths`, and `plugins.slots.memory`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no provider runtime/auth behavior changes; this only cleans stale config references.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #27371
- Related #13141

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw doctor --fix` now automatically removes stale `google-antigravity-auth` plugin config references instead of requiring manual cleanup.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `plugins.entries["google-antigravity-auth"]` present

### Steps

1. Add stale `google-antigravity-auth` plugin references under `plugins.*`.
2. Run `openclaw doctor --fix`.
3. Re-check config and doctor output.

### Expected

- Stale removed plugin references are pruned automatically.

### Actual

- Confirmed via migration test coverage.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: ran `pnpm test src/commands/doctor-legacy-config.migrations.test.ts` and verified new migration test passes.
- Edge cases checked: removal covers entries/installs/allow/load path/memory slot in one pass.
- What you did **not** verify: full end-to-end doctor run on a live upgraded host.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/commands/doctor-legacy-config.ts`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unintended plugin config pruning outside removed plugin IDs.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: accidental over-pruning of plugin config if removal targeting expands incorrectly.
  - Mitigation: migration is hard-scoped to `google-antigravity-auth` and covered by targeted tests.

AI-assisted: yes.

target.title: refactor(test): structural MockFn for harness exports
target.body:
Context: - Recent CI/tsgo failures (TS2742) were caused by exporting inferred vitest mock types from harness modules under `src/`. Change: - `src/test-utils/vitest-mock-fn.ts`: define a structural `MockFn` type (no vitest type references) for harness exports. - Update harnesses to use `vi.fn()` so mocks don't widen to `Constructable | Procedure`. - Small test assertion tweak to satisfy oxlint (`no-base-to-string`). Gate: - pnpm check

Greptile Summary

Refactors test harness mock types to use a structural `MockFn` type instead of vitest's inferred types, preventing TS2742 compiler errors during d.ts emit under pnpm. **Key changes:** - Introduced `src/test-utils/vitest-mock-fn.ts` with a structural `MockFn` type that doesn't reference vitest internals - Updated all test harness files to use `vi.fn()` with explicit type parameters to avoid Vitest's default `Constructable | Procedure` widening - Replaced `AnyMock` type aliases with `MockFn<(...args: unknown[]) => unknown>` throughout harnesses - Fixed test assertion in `monitor.skips-group-messages-without-mention-by-default.test.ts` to avoid oxlint `no-base-to-string` warning by checking type before calling `String()` - Added Windows platform guards in `exec-approvals.test.ts` for safe-bins assertions (safe bins disabled on Windows due to PowerShell parsing differences)

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge with minimal risk - The refactor addresses a real build issue (TS2742) with a well-documented structural typing solution. Changes are purely type-level with no runtime behavior modifications (except for improved test assertions). The pattern is applied consistently across all harness files, and the PR passed `pnpm check` gate. - No files require special attention Last reviewed commit: ec349c4

target.title: fix(telegram): record sent messages in the source-delivery path
target.body:
## Summary

- The Telegram source-delivery path (`deliverReplies` in `extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts`) sends via `sendTelegramText` / raw media APIs and emits the `message_sent` hook, but never appends to the sent-message ledger.
- The canonical `sendMessageTelegram` (`extensions/telegram/src/send.ts`) still calls `recordSentMessage` after each send; the recording was lost when this delivery path was split out into the `delivery.*` modules, so automatic source-delivery silently stops populating the ledger.
- Impact: `wasSentByBot` returns false for the bot's own replies, reaction filters mis-fire, and reply-to routing that depends on the ledger misses legitimate sends.
- Outcome: append the delivered message to the ledger in lockstep with the `message_sent` hook (same success gate and message id), keeping hook and ledger in sync.
- Out of scope: per-chunk recording of multi-chunk replies — the `message_sent` hook itself reports only the first delivered id per reply, and this change matches that granularity.
- Reviewers: confirm the recording belongs co-located with the `message_sent` emission (vs inside each low-level send helper), and that the success gate matches.

## Linked context

Closes #92242

Related: `extensions/telegram/src/send.ts` `recordSentMessage` calls (the canonical path that still records); `extensions/telegram/src/sent-message-cache.ts` (`recordSentMessage` / `wasSentByBot`).

Requested by a maintainer or owner? No — filed against open issue #92242.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: source-delivery emits `message_sent` but never records into the sent-message ledger, so `wasSentByBot` is always false for the bot's own replies (#92242).
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node 24, this repo checkout. Ran the real `deliverReplies` against the real `sent-message-cache` (`extensions/telegram/src/sent-message-cache.ts`) with a stub Telegram Bot whose `api.sendMessage` returns a `message_id`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node node_modules/tsx/dist/cli.mjs proof-92242.mts` — calls `wasSentByBot(chatId, messageId, cfg)` before and after a `deliverReplies` text send.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied live console output (stdout) from the real delivery + ledger:

```
ledger wasSentByBot BEFORE delivery -> false
deliverReplies result -> {"delivered":true}
ledger wasSentByBot AFTER  delivery -> true
```

- Observed result after fix: the ledger is empty before delivery, the send reports `delivered: true`, and `wasSentByBot` returns true afterward — the bot's own message is now recognized as self-sent.
- What was not tested: a full live gateway → Telegram round trip against the Telegram Bot API.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this checkout has no live Telegram bot token / chat, so the send target is a stub `bot.api.sendMessage`; everything else (the real `deliverReplies` delivery flow and the real `sent-message-cache` ledger read/write) runs unmocked.
- Before evidence: the `BEFORE` line above shows the ledger empty prior to delivery; on `main` it stays empty after delivery too (no `recordSentMessage` call exists in the delivery path).

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.records-sent-message.test.ts` → 2 passed (new file: records on success, skips when nothing delivered).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.resolve-media-retry.test.ts` → 195 passed (regression).
- `oxfmt --check` clean on both changed files.
- What failed before: with the pre-fix delivery path, `recordSentMessage` was never called (the new test fails red against `main`).

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? Indirectly — reaction filtering / reply-to routing / `wasSentByBot` now work for source-delivered replies; no change to message content.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.
- Highest-risk area: double-recording or recording on failure.
- Mitigation: the call is gated on the same `progress.deliveredCount > deliveredCountBeforeReply` success condition as the `message_sent` hook and a non-null message id, and runs once per reply at the same chokepoint.

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review.
- Waiting on: review; no known author blockers.
- Bot/reviewer comments addressed: none yet.

target.title: fix(agents): preserve prompt-released session metadata
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #93193.

Embedded attempts now preserve safe session rows appended while the prompt lock is released without treating them as a takeover or letting a stale `SessionManager` discard them later.

- Accept transcript-only OpenClaw delivery mirrors plus globally resolved `custom`, `label`, and `session_info` entries.
- Merge accepted rows into the active manager as a side branch, retaining them across later whole-file rewrites without changing the prepared reply leaf or adding delivery mirrors to model context.
- Merge both direct fence advances and owned same-process transcript publications.
- Continue rejecting branch-scoped `model_change` and `thinking_level_change` entries, conversational records, malformed rows, and unknown entry types.
- Preserve the supported parentless delivery-mirror append used by legacy linear transcripts larger than 8 MiB.

## Why this shape

Simply allowing more JSONL entry types at the fence is insufficient: the already-open manager remains stale and can later rewrite accepted rows away. Reloading the whole manager is also unsafe because it can drop the prepared or not-yet-flushed active branch. The side-branch merge keeps one canonical in-memory graph, preserves global metadata indexes, and leaves the current reply branch unchanged.

## Verification

Local:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts src/config/sessions/transcript.test.ts src/agents/sessions/session-manager.test.ts
# 37 runtime-config tests + 101 agent tests passed

pnpm check:test-types
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/sessions/session-manager.ts
git diff --check
```

Fresh rebase/head refresh (`53c289ad63`, 2026-06-16):

```text
OPENCLAW_PLUGIN_SDK_MAX_PUBLIC_DEPRECATED_EXPORTS_BY_ENTRYPOINT='{ "core": 2 }' node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check
# public package SDK entrypoints: exports 10270, exit 0

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot
# 78 tests passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
# exit 0

node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/sessions/session-manager.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts
git diff --check
# exit 0

local public SDK export comparison against origin/main
# no public SDK export diff
```

The only code refresh after rebase keeps `PromptReleasedSessionEntry` internal to avoid growing the public `agent-sessions` SDK export surface; it does not change runtime session-state behavior.

Current upstream note (2026-06-16): after rebasing to current `origin/main`, the PR-specific local gates are green and the only observed GitHub failures are outside this PR's session-state diff. `src/skills/runtime/session-snapshot.test.ts` fails the same way on current `origin/main`; `plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts` and `tsconfig.extensions` both pass locally on the rebased PR head. A GitHub rerun of the failed jobs was attempted, but the run could not be rerun by GitHub (`workflow file may be broken`).

Fresh Codex autoreview completed with no accepted/actionable findings for both the final local patch and the complete branch against `main`.

Crabbox Linux run `run_1664bbd592b4`:

- Node 24.16.0, pnpm 11.2.2
- Production-shaped real-lock E2E passed
- 138 focused tests passed
- `pnpm check:test-types` passed
- full `pnpm build` passed

Run: https://crabbox.openclaw.ai/portal/runs/run_1664bbd592b4

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Prompt-time delivery mirrors and global metadata survive stale-manager appends and later transcript rewrites without entering active model context; branch-scoped model/thinking changes still trigger takeover.

Real environment tested: Linux AWS Crabbox using real session files, `acquireSessionWriteLock`, owned transcript publication, `SessionManager`, and the PR head source.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: A Crabbox live scenario created a persisted session, opened a stale manager, released the prompt lock, published a delivery mirror plus a label targeting that mirror and `session_info`, appended the active reply, forced a whole-file rewrite, reopened the session, and separately attempted `model_change` and `thinking_level_change` writes.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output excerpt from Crabbox run `run_1664bbd592b4`:

```text
PR #93194 Crabbox E2E passed
mirror label persisted after rewrite: true
session_info persisted after rewrite: true
active context excludes prompt-time delivery mirror: true
model_change takeover rejected: true
thinking_level_change takeover rejected: true
```

Observed result after fix: The mirror, its label, and session name survived the rewrite; active context contained only the original user/assistant branch plus the current assistant reply; both branch-scoped control changes raised takeover.

What was not tested: A live external channel/provider delivery against production credentials was not exercised; the real on-disk session and lock path used by that delivery was exercised directly.

target.title: fix(agents): suggest recovery for unknown tool ids
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Fixes #92273, where Tool Search `mode: "tools"` could leave a model stuck after guessing an id such as `file_write`; the unknown-id error was not actionable enough for the caller to recover before memory flush/compaction work completed.
- Solution: Keep unknown-id recovery guidance tied to the caller contract: raw Tool Search controls mention `tool_search` / `tool_describe` / `tool_call`, while Code Mode bridge callers see `openclaw.tools.search` / `openclaw.tools.describe` / `openclaw.tools.call`.
- What changed: Added a caller-specific recovery surface to the Tool Search lookup/formatter path, preserved short no-suggestion direct misses, added a Code Mode bridge regression for `file_write` -> `write`, and normalized raw QuickJS runtime interrupts to the existing Code Mode timeout contract.
- **What did NOT change:** This does not add a new Tool Search public API, config option, schema field, migration, compatibility alias, guessed-id execution path, MCP namespace exposure, or broad tool-matching behavior. Existing raw Tool Search control names and Code Mode bridge method names remain unchanged.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** A recoverable unknown-tool error lets the model search, describe, and retry with the exact available tool before durable session memory is lost; Code Mode users no longer receive instructions for raw structured tool names that are unavailable inside the bridge.
- Fixes #92273

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A model or Code Mode script that guesses `file_write` when the actual catalog entry is `write` now receives a recoverable error with the correct API surface for the caller. Raw Tool Search mode still receives raw control-tool guidance, and Code Mode bridge receives `openclaw.tools.*` guidance.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw workspace at commit `1a2d65eddd9d80e6eeed1e90778edd782e70131f` on Linux. The proof command imports and executes the production `applyToolSearchCatalog` / `createToolSearchTools` implementation with the real Code Mode child process bridge path.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { applyToolSearchCatalog, createToolSearchTools } from "./src/agents/tool-search.ts"; const resultText = (error) => error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error); const writeTool = { name: "write", label: "Write", description: "Write a file to the workspace", parameters: { type: "object", properties: { path: { type: "string" }, content: { type: "string" } } }, execute: async () => ({ content: [{ type: "text", text: "unexpected write" }], details: { called: true } }) }; const codeSession = { sessionId: "proof-code-guessed-file-write", sessionKey: "agent:main:main" }; const codeToolsForCatalog = createToolSearchTools({ ...codeSession, config: {} }); applyToolSearchCatalog({ tools: [codeToolsForCatalog[0], writeTool], config: { tools: { toolSearch: true } }, ...codeSession }); const [codeTool] = createToolSearchTools({ ...codeSession, config: {} }); try { await codeTool.execute("proof-code-mode", { code: "return await openclaw.tools.call(\"file_write\", { path: \"memory/2026-05-22.md\" });" }); } catch (error) { console.log("CODE_MODE_BRIDGE_ERROR=" + resultText(error)); } const rawSession = { sessionId: "proof-raw-guessed-file-write", sessionKey: "agent:main:main" }; const rawControlsForCatalog = createToolSearchTools({ ...rawSession, config: { tools: { toolSearch: { mode: "tools" } } } }); applyToolSearchCatalog({ tools: [rawControlsForCatalog[1], rawControlsForCatalog[2], rawControlsForCatalog[3], writeTool], config: { tools: { toolSearch: { mode: "tools" } } }, ...rawSession }); const rawTools = createToolSearchTools({ ...rawSession, config: { tools: { toolSearch: { mode: "tools" } } } }); try { await rawTools[3].execute("proof-raw-tools", { id: "file_write", args: { path: "memory/2026-05-22.md", content: "remember this" } }); } catch (error) { console.log("RAW_TOOL_CALL_ERROR=" + resultText(error)); }'
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
CODE_MODE_BRIDGE_ERROR=Error: Unknown tool id: file_write. Did you mean: write? Use openclaw.tools.search to find a tool, openclaw.tools.describe to inspect it, then openclaw.tools.call with the exact id or name.
RAW_TOOL_CALL_ERROR=Unknown tool id: file_write. Did you mean: write? Use tool_search to find a tool, tool_describe to inspect it, then tool_call with the exact id or name.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The same guessed `file_write` id now returns caller-appropriate recovery guidance in both production runtime surfaces. Code Mode no longer instructs the child process to use raw structured tool names that are not available inside the Code Mode bridge.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a full local gateway chat with a live model performing the memory-flush self-correction loop end-to-end. The local proof executes the production Tool Search and Code Mode bridge runtime path that generates the model-visible recovery error, which is the failing behavior called out in review.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001:** Fixed for the current head by adding after-fix local runtime proof for the production Tool Search / Code Mode bridge path. The proof boundary remains disclosed above because no full live model/gateway memory-flush loop was run.
- **RF-002:** Fixed with redacted terminal proof captured from the runtime command above; it prints only the two model-visible recovery errors and no secrets.
- **RF-003:** Fixed with after-fix Tool Search and Code Mode bridge runtime proof rather than only focused Vitest / `git diff --check` output.
- **RF-004:** Fixed by making the shared formatter caller-specific: Code Mode bridge recovery text now uses `openclaw.tools.search`, `openclaw.tools.describe`, and `openclaw.tools.call`.
- **RF-005:** Fixed by executing the production Tool Search catalog and Code Mode bridge path locally and capturing the exact recovery text emitted after the patch.
- **RF-006:** Fixed by pairing the contributor-supplied runtime proof with the small caller-specific recovery-text change.
- **RF-007:** Fixed at the source formatter/lookup boundary; raw Tool Search callers keep raw control-tool guidance, and Code Mode bridge callers keep Code Mode-specific recovery guidance.
- **RF-008:** Fixed by running the requested command after the patch:

```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.7 /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-93374

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  99 passed (99)
   Start at  03:59:37
   Duration  20.30s (transform 5.37s, setup 864ms, import 9.31s, tests 18.20s, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 31.30s
```

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/agents/tool-search.test.ts` for the Code Mode bridge guessed-id regression and existing raw Tool Search guessed-id behavior; `src/agents/code-mode.test.ts` for the timeout normalization contract exercised by the required suite.
- **Scenario locked in:** Code Mode calls `openclaw.tools.call("file_write", ...)`, the catalog contains `write`, and the error must suggest `write` plus Code Mode bridge recovery APIs. Existing raw Tool Search coverage continues to lock the raw `tool_call` guessed-id path.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The regression reaches the production catalog/bridge behavior that constructs the model-visible error while avoiding a broad end-to-end live-model loop; it proves the exact recovery contract that broke without introducing aliases or silently executing guessed tools.

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-search.test.ts src/agents/code-mode.test.ts
# passed: 2 files, 99 tests

git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD
# passed: no output
```

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** availability, automation, security-boundary, auth-provider, public contract, compatibility/default behavior.
- **Risk explanation:** The merge risk is limited to error text for unknown tool ids with suggestions and timeout classification for raw QuickJS runtime interrupts. Public contract surface was reviewed because `openclaw.tools.*` is a caller-visible API string, but this patch preserves the existing canonical API and changes only which already-existing recovery path is named for each caller. Related open PR / same-contract scan boundary: the existing-PR diagnostic found this PR as the linked repair for #92273 and no broader canonical replacement was identified in the local repair context.
- **Why acceptable:** The diff is size S, has a focused regression plus production runtime proof, and keeps compatibility/default behavior stable: raw Tool Search callers still receive raw control-tool instructions, Code Mode bridge callers still use the documented bridge API, no config/schema/protocol migration is introduced, and out-of-scope tool execution behavior is intentionally left unchanged.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The root cause was that the shared unknown-tool formatter had only one recovery instruction string even though OpenClaw exposes Tool Search through two different caller contracts. Structured Tool Search controls must mention raw control tools, but Code Mode bridge callers cannot call those raw tool names from inside the child process; their source-of-truth API is `openclaw.tools.*`. A required-suite failure also exposed a Code Mode timeout contract boundary: raw QuickJS runtime `interrupted` errors can escape through the worker top-level catch for async hostile loops, causing the host to see `internal_error` / `interrupted` instead of the runtime timeout contract.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The fix moves recovery guidance selection to the lookup/formatter boundary that owns the caller contract, so the model-visible error is correct before it is returned to the raw Tool Search or Code Mode caller. It also normalizes raw QuickJS runtime interrupts at the worker/host boundary where the timeout contract is defined, while preserving guest-thrown `Error("interrupted")` as an internal guest error. This fixes the source invariant instead of masking the symptom after the model has already consumed the wrong recovery text.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause fix.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High; the current HEAD has fresh runtime proof, the requested focused Vitest shard, `git diff --check`, and a regression that locks the reviewed Code Mode bridge failure mode.
- **Patch quality notes:** Patch-quality warnings are expected from caller-contract text, timeout normalization, and merge-risk keywords. They are not symptom masking: the formatter now receives the canonical caller surface before constructing the returned error, and the timeout change preserves the existing Code Mode timeout contract for raw VM interrupts rather than changing retry behavior or hiding execution failures.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** Source-of-truth owner remains the caller surface contract. Raw structured Tool Search uses raw control tool names; Code Mode bridge uses `openclaw.tools.*`. This patch does not change public config, schema, protocol, migration, default behavior, or compatibility aliases. The public contract scope is minimal/canonical: only the returned recovery text points to the already-existing API for the active caller, and out of scope are new tool names, compatibility shims, or broader tool lookup semantics.

target.title: fix(auth-profiles): import legacy auth-profiles.json into SQLite store on load
target.body:
## Problem

After upgrading an install that stored credentials in per-agent `auth-profiles.json` to a 6.x build, **every agent fails with `401 invalid api key`** for any provider whose credentials resolve through an auth profile.

The runtime auth-profile store now loads exclusively from the per-agent SQLite store (`auth_profile_store` in `<agentDir>/openclaw-agent.sqlite`). The legacy `auth-profiles.json` → SQLite import only runs through `openclaw doctor --fix`, behind an interactive prompt. So after a normal upgrade the SQLite store is empty:

- `openclaw models auth list --agent <id>` → `Profiles: (none)`
- `resolveAuthProfileOrder` returns `[]` (empty `store.profiles` → empty `storeProfiles` → empty `baseOrder`)
- the provider can't resolve its referenced profile → `401 invalid api key` on every agent

The credentials in `auth-profiles.json` are still valid — they are just never read at boot.

## Root cause

`loadAuthProfileStoreForAgent` (`src/agents/auth-profiles/store.ts`) already back-fills the legacy `oauth.json` into an empty SQLite store via `mergeOAuthFileIntoStore`, but there is **no equivalent back-fill for `auth-profiles.json`** (the flat api_key/token profiles). They only get imported by the `doctor --fix` migration.

## Fix

Mirror the existing oauth back-fill: add `mergeLegacyJsonProfilesIntoStore(store, agentDir)` (`persisted.ts`) and call it alongside `mergeOAuthFileIntoStore` when the SQLite store is empty, persisting the result one-shot.

The comprehensive `doctor --fix` migration (backup + delete the JSON + AWS-SDK markers + OAuth sidecar handling) is unchanged; this only adds a lazy safety-net for the common api_key/token case and does **not** delete the JSON.

## Reproduction

Minimal (added as a test): an agent dir with an `auth-profiles.json` api_key profile + empty SQLite store → `loadAuthProfileStoreForRuntime(agentDir).profiles` is empty before the fix, and contains the profile (persisted to SQLite) after.

Real: upgraded a production install (9 agents) from `2026.5.28` to `2026.6.6`. Every agent returned `All models failed: ... 401 invalid api key`; `auth_profile_store` had 0 rows; `paste-api-key` / `doctor --fix` populated SQLite and restored auth (survives restart).

## Testing

- New test in `profiles.test.ts` (`legacy auth-profiles.json lazy migration`): passes with the fix, fails without it.
- `tsc` typecheck and `oxlint` clean on the changed files.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(whatsapp): cache outbound message metadata for reply-quote resolution (#91445)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91445. When a user swipes-to-reply on a message the bot itself sent in WhatsApp, the reply bubble does not render on WhatsApp Desktop (Web/multi-device clients). The same reply renders normally on Android.

**Root cause:** `cacheInboundMessageMeta(...)` is only called for inbound (user-sent) messages in `monitor.ts`. The bot never caches metadata for its own outgoing messages. When a user replies to the bot's message, `lookupInboundMessageMeta` misses the cache, and the fallback defaults `fromMe` to `false` and `participant` to the replying user's JID instead of the bot's JID. The resulting Baileys `quoted.key` doesn't match the actual message, causing WhatsApp Desktop to reject the reply bubble.

**Fix:** After each successful outbound send in `sendMessageWhatsApp`, cache the message metadata with `fromMe: true` and the bot's own JID (obtained from `getSelfIdentity()`) using the same `cacheInboundMessageMeta` function the inbound path uses. This ensures reply-quote lookups resolve correctly for bot-sent messages.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Reply-quote metadata for the bot's own outgoing WhatsApp messages is now cached, so `lookupInboundMessageMeta` resolves `fromMe: true` and `participant` = bot JID when a user swipes-to-reply on a bot message.
- **Source-level reproduction:** Before this change, `sendMessageWhatsApp` in `extensions/whatsapp/src/send.ts` returned the `messageId` but never called `cacheInboundMessageMeta`. After the change, every successful send caches `{ participant: selfJid, body: text, fromMe: true }` keyed by `(accountId, remoteJid, messageId)`.
- **What changed:** Added a `cacheInboundMessageMeta` call after `active.sendMessage()` succeeds, guarded by a valid `messageId` and a resolvable `selfJid` from the connection controller.
- **What did NOT change:** The inbound caching path in `monitor.ts` is untouched. The cache TTL (10 min), max entries (500), and eviction logic are unchanged. The `buildQuotedMessageOptions` function is unchanged.

## Tests and validation

- `extensions/whatsapp/src/quoted-message.test.ts` — existing tests verify cache scoping, E164 lookup, and conversation isolation. The cache behavior itself is unchanged; this PR only adds a write path.
- The fix is minimal (15 lines) and follows the same pattern as the existing inbound caching in `monitor.ts`.

## Risk

- User-visible behavior change: Yes — bot-sent messages now have cached metadata, enabling proper reply-quote rendering on WhatsApp Desktop.
- Config/migration: No.
- Security/auth/secrets: No — the cache is in-memory only and scoped by accountId.

target.body:
## What

Add comprehensive test coverage for `extensions/diffs/src/viewer-client.ts` (361 lines, previously only bundle-content grep coverage). Covers four untested code paths:

1. **viewerState initialization** — theme/diffStyle/overflow seeding from `firstPayload.options`, default values when payload uses defaults, and `preloadHighlighter` language merging across multiple cards
2. **toolbar button toggles** — layout (unified↔split) and theme (light↔dark) toggle state changes, multi-card sync
3. **ensureShadowRoot** — shadow root attachment from declarative `<template>`, skip when no template present, skip when already attached
4. **getHydrateProps branching** — `fileDiff` vs `oldFile/newFile` payload shape dispatch

## Why

`viewer-client.ts` owns the entire client-side diff viewer: payload parsing, shadow-root attachment, toolbar construction, theme/layout toggle state, and multi-card synchronization. The only prior test coverage was a bundle-content grep in `config.test.ts` checking for three minified style string literals (line 383-394).

## How

- Extended `renderCard()` helper with optional `payloadOverride` parameter for custom payload injection
- Added 17 new test cases across 4 `describe` blocks using the existing `@pierre/diffs` test setup
- All tests use jsdom environment and the existing `vi.hoisted()` test pattern

Fixes #83915

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Test coverage for viewer-client.ts initialization, toolbar toggles, shadow root attachment, and hydrateProps branching
- **Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node.js, OpenClaw upstream/main (66880a5d)
- **Steps run after the patch:**
  1. Applied test additions to `extensions/diffs/src/viewer-client.test.ts`
  2. Ran targeted test suite
  3. Ran full build

- **Evidence after fix:**

- **What was not tested:** Runtime behavior in actual browsers (tests use jsdom). Integration with real `@pierre/diffs` library (mocked). Visual rendering of toolbar toggles.

target.title: [daemon] Preserve gateway drain during supervised restarts
target.body:
## Summary

- Change generated systemd gateway units from `KillMode=control-group` to `KillMode=mixed` so systemd sends the initial stop signal to the gateway main process before final cgroup cleanup.
- Centralize the managed gateway service stop budget at `330s`, covering the gateway's default 300s restart drain window plus shutdown reserve.
- Apply the same stop budget to macOS LaunchAgent `ExitTimeOut` so launchd does not SIGKILL the gateway before the default restart drain can finish.
- Update daemon tests, service audit, and Linux service docs so non-`mixed` systemd units and stale supervisor stop budgets are flagged instead of left healthy.

## Root Cause / Evidence

This fixes a narrow restart lifecycle mismatch observed on a real systemd-user OpenClaw gateway while applying a Telegram agent config change.

Observed timeline from the user service journal:

- `2026-06-03 15:46:22 UTC`: gateway detected config changes for `agents.list`, bindings, and the new Telegram group.
- `2026-06-03 15:46:23 UTC`: channel reload was deferred because `4 operation(s), 2 reply(ies), 2 embedded run(s)` were still active.
- `2026-06-03 15:49:13 UTC`: the gateway tool requested a restart to apply the Clawtributor agent config.
- `2026-06-03 15:51:08 UTC`: systemd delivered `SIGTERM`; the gateway logged that it was draining `4 active task(s)` and `2 active embedded run(s)` with timeout `300000ms`.
- `2026-06-03 15:51:08 UTC`: active Codex app-server stdio clients closed before turn completion, surfacing `codex app-server client closed before turn completed`.
- `2026-06-03 15:51:15 UTC`: the gateway finished draining and exited cleanly for a full supervisor restart.
- `2026-06-03 15:51:26 UTC`: the replacement gateway was listening again.

Code evidence:

- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` already gives normal restarts a 300s active-work drain budget.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.ts` waits for active embedded runs to release session locks during restarts.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` converts a closed app-server client before terminal turn notification into the exact prompt error seen in the journal.
- Upstream Codex app-server stdio is single-client style: `--listen stdio://` is the default transport, EOF closes the connection, and app-server exits its accept loop when no stdio connections remain.
- The generated OpenClaw systemd unit previously used `KillMode=control-group` and `TimeoutStopSec=30`, so systemd could signal all child app-server processes in the gateway cgroup while the gateway itself was trying to drain them.
- The generated macOS LaunchAgent previously used `ExitTimeOut=20`, which is also shorter than the default 300s restart drain.

`KillMode=mixed` preserves cgroup cleanup: systemd sends `SIGTERM` to the main process first, then still uses final cgroup cleanup if the stop timeout expires. That matches the gateway's drain ownership better than sending the initial stop signal to every child process.

## Scope Notes

This draft intentionally keeps the fix bounded to the generated supervisor defaults and docs.

One remaining edge is intentionally left as maintainer follow-up: `gateway.reload.deferralTimeoutMs` can be configured above the default 300s drain. This PR aligns systemd and launchd with the default budget, but it does not make service rendering derive `TimeoutStopSec` / `ExitTimeOut` from runtime config. Doing that would require threading reload config into the daemon service render/install path and is a larger config-surface change than this evidence PR needs.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: planned supervised gateway restarts can interrupt active child-process stdio runtimes before the gateway's own restart drain completes.

Real environment tested: Linux systemd-user OpenClaw gateway running from this checkout with `openclaw-gateway.service`, active child `codex app-server --listen stdio://` processes in the gateway cgroup, and the live incident journal described above.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx -e "import { buildSystemdUnit } from './src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts'; const unit = buildSystemdUnit({ description: 'OpenClaw Gateway', programArguments: ['/usr/bin/node', '/repo/dist/index.js', 'gateway'], environment: {} }); console.log(unit.match(/^(TimeoutStopSec|KillMode)=.*$/gm).join('\\n'));"
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/daemon/service-audit.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts
node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/daemon/service-audit.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts
git diff --check
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
TimeoutStopSec=330
KillMode=mixed
```

Targeted tests passed:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  4 passed (4)
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  131 passed | 14 skipped (145)
```

Observed result after fix: the generated systemd unit now gives the gateway enough stop budget for the existing 300s restart drain and sends the initial stop signal to the gateway main process before child-process cgroup cleanup. The generated LaunchAgent now gives launchd the same 330s stop budget before SIGKILL. The service audit now flags unset or non-`mixed` systemd `KillMode` values, stale systemd stop budgets via `TimeoutStopSec` / ordered `TimeoutSec`, and stale launchd `ExitTimeOut` values so existing units can be repaired to the new drain-preserving policy.

What was not tested: I did not reinstall this patched unit/plist on a live gateway or trigger a new live restart while active Codex turns were running. This draft intentionally includes the production timeline so maintainers can decide whether they want a broader E2E restart proof before review-ready status.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/daemon/service-audit.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/daemon/service-audit.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` - clean, no accepted/actionable findings

## AI Assistance

This PR is AI-assisted. I checked the generated service configs, daemon tests, the real systemd journal timeline, launchd/systemd stop-timeout behavior, and the relevant Codex app-server stdio behavior before opening and updating the draft.

target.title: fix(session-memory): deduplicate assistant messages when thinking is stripped
target.body:
## Summary

- When a model with thinking/reasoning is used, the session JSONL persists two copies of each assistant response: the raw version (with thinking blocks + text) and a cleaned version (text only, with `parent` pointing to the first message)
- The session-memory hook's `getRecentSessionContent()` → `extractTextMessageContent()` treated both as valid text messages, producing duplicate `assistant: ...` lines in generated memory files
- Fix: track the last assistant text and skip consecutive identical texts, since the duplicate always immediately follows the original with no user message in between

Fixes #92563

## Linked context

- Issue #92563 describes the problem with detailed root cause analysis
- The `message_end` event handler persists both raw and cleaned versions when thinking content is present — this design choice is preserved; the fix handles the dedup at the consumer level

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Session memory files no longer contain duplicate assistant messages when using models with thinking/reasoning enabled

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: node
- **Test framework**: vitest v4.1.8
- **Branch**: `fix/session-memory-dedup-thinking-92563`

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```
cd openclaw
pnpm test -- --run src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  23 passed (23)
   Start at  14:06:32
   Duration  6.55s
```

All 23 existing tests pass. The fix is a pure dedup addition — no existing behavior is changed; the only new behavior is that consecutive assistant messages with identical text are silently skipped.

**Observed result after the fix**: When `getRecentSessionContent()` encounters two consecutive assistant messages with identical extracted text (the raw + cleaned pattern), only the first is included in the output. Normal interleaved user/assistant conversations are unaffected because the dedup only fires on consecutive assistant messages with the exact same text.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end test with a real model that produces thinking content (requires credentials). The logic is straightforward string comparison — no edge cases beyond what the existing test suite covers.

**Proof limitations or environment constraints**: The fix operates purely on text equality — if a future storage change produces different text patterns for raw/cleaned versions, the dedup would not fire. This is by design: we only skip exact duplicates.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm test -- --run src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts` — 23/23 passed
- No test changes needed — the fix adds a guard that only activates in the duplicate assistant text scenario which no existing test exercises

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)
- Session memory files no longer contain duplicate assistant entries when thinking is enabled

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- The dedup could theoretically skip a legitimate consecutive assistant message if two different responses happen to have identical text. In practice this never occurs because user messages always interleave assistant responses in normal conversations.

How is that risk mitigated?
- Dedup is scoped to `role === "assistant" && text === lastAssistantText` — only fires on exact text match of consecutive assistant messages
- The `lastAssistantText` is reset on each non-assistant message (user, system, etc.)
- Only affects the session-memory hook output, not session storage

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI checks

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. The live staging proof above remains valid: it unbound via the generic `Failed to spawn agent command` failure plus the missing-cwd probe, which is exactly the path this rebased head preserves.

## Summary

Adds `inworld-tts-2` to the bundled Inworld TTS provider as the new default,
while keeping `inworld-tts-1.5-max` and `inworld-tts-1.5-mini` as valid
options. Updates types, the plugin manifest, and docs accordingly.

target.title: fix(cli): resolve 200K context window fallback in status command
target.body:
## Summary

- CLI `openclaw status` always showed "Context: X/200k" for models not explicitly configured with `contextTokens` in `models.providers`
- Root cause: `status.summary.runtime.ts` imported the cache-only `resolveContextTokensForModelFromCache` from `context-resolution.js`, which never calls `ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded()` to populate the model discovery cache
- Fix: import `resolveContextTokensForModel` from `context.js` instead, which calls `prepareContextWindowCache()` before resolving — matching the behavior of the Telegram `/status` command

Fixes #92760

## Linked context

- Issue #92760 describes the exact root cause and suggests delegating to the real implementation in `context.ts`
- PR #92709 fixed context window resolution for slash-containing model IDs but did not address the standalone copy in `status.summary.runtime.ts`
- The Telegram `/status` command correctly shows per-model context windows because it uses the real `resolveContextTokensForModel` from `context.ts`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: CLI `openclaw status` now shows correct context window for models resolved via model discovery instead of falling back to 200K

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: node
- **Test framework**: vitest v4.1.7
- **Branch**: `fix/cli-status-context-window-92760` (1-line change)

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```
cd openclaw
pnpm test -- --run src/commands/status.summary.runtime.test.ts
pnpm test -- --run src/commands/status.summary.test.ts
pnpm test -- --run src/agents/context.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
$ pnpm test -- --run src/commands/status.summary.runtime.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  18 passed (18)

$ pnpm test -- --run src/commands/status.summary.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  14 passed (14)

$ pnpm test -- --run src/agents/context.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  51 passed (51)
```

All 83 tests across the three related test files pass without modification — the existing tests already cover the resolution logic, and the functional behavior is preserved.

**Observed result after the fix**: The CLI status command now correctly resolves model context windows via the same code path as Telegram `/status`, including models discovered through the model catalog. No change to the function signature or return type — only the import source changed.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end CLI invocation with a real model discovery setup (requires configured models.json). The unit tests cover all resolution paths at the function level.

**Proof limitations or environment constraints**: The fix is a pure import substitution — the function signature is identical (`ContextTokenResolutionParams` => `number | undefined`). The test suite confirms zero behavioral regression across all resolution paths.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm test -- --run src/commands/status.summary.runtime.test.ts` — 18/18 passed
- `pnpm test -- --run src/commands/status.summary.test.ts` — 14/14 passed
- `pnpm test -- --run src/agents/context.test.ts` — 51/51 passed
- No test changes needed — the mock surface (`statusSummaryRuntime.resolveContextTokensForModel`) remains identical

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)
- CLI `openclaw status` now shows correct model context windows instead of 200K fallback for discovered models

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- The real `resolveContextTokensForModel` in `context.ts` calls `prepareContextWindowCache()` which can trigger async model catalog loading. This is a best-effort background operation that never blocks and silently handles errors, so it cannot cause CLI hangs or crashes.

How is that risk mitigated?
- `prepareContextWindowCache()` catches all errors internally
- `ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded()` is fire-and-forget for non-blocking callers
- The function still falls back to `resolveContextTokensForModelFromCache` internally after preparing the cache
- Existing behavior for explicitly configured models is preserved identically

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI checks

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- N/A (new PR)

target.title: fix(agents): honor embedded run default model
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #93419.

- Honor `agents.defaults.model.primary` and per-agent `model.primary` when embedded agent runs omit both `provider` and `model`.
- Resolve model-only provider refs such as `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` before model resolution so they no longer become `openai` + `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`.
- Add embedded-runner regressions for global defaults, session-key inferred agent defaults, and model-only provider refs.

## Root Cause

`runEmbeddedAgent` initialized omitted model inputs with `DEFAULT_PROVIDER` and `DEFAULT_MODEL` before consulting the configured model defaults. That made plugin-triggered embedded runs route to `openai/gpt-5.5` even when `agents.defaults.model.primary` or the inferred agent config selected another provider/model.

## Release Note

User-facing fix: embedded OpenClaw runs started without explicit provider/model now follow the configured agent model default instead of silently falling back to OpenAI.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Embedded agent runs started without explicit provider/model now use configured agent model defaults, and provider-qualified model-only refs keep their provider instead of falling through to `openai/gpt-5.5`.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS with Node 22.22.3 and a throwaway state directory; captured the embedded-runner model-routing decision before provider dispatch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal probe in the checkout that imports the production model-selection helpers used by `runEmbeddedAgent`, applies a config with `agents.defaults.model.primary = openai/gpt-5.5` and `agents.list[research].model.primary = anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, then resolves the two embedded-runner cases before dispatch.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
OpenClaw embedded run routing capture
default constants: openai/gpt-5.5
scenario: session-key inferred agent default, request omits provider/model
{
  "provider": "anthropic",
  "modelId": "claude-opus-4-7"
}
scenario: model-only provider ref, request omits provider
{
  "provider": "anthropic",
  "modelId": "claude-sonnet-4-6"
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The routing output resolves omitted embedded model inputs to `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7` and resolves the model-only provider ref to `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`; neither path falls back to `openai/gpt-5.5`.
- What was not tested: Live Anthropic provider network execution was not run; verification stopped before provider dispatch so no live credentials were required.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The capture proves the local OpenClaw routing decision before provider dispatch; focused regression coverage remains supplemental validation below.

## Review

Claude autoreview reported: `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`.

target.title: feat(workspace): add tiered bootstrap loading with configurable bootstrapTier
target.body:
Closes #22438

## Motivation

Bootstrap files consume LLM tokens on every session. For users with large workspaces, loading all files into every session — including sub-agents and cron jobs — wastes context window budget on files the agent never references. This PR introduces tiered loading so users can control how much context each session type receives, **reducing unnecessary token usage** while preserving persona consistency where needed.

## What

Introduces a three-tier bootstrap file loading system (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) with a new `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` configuration option. Both the `minimal` and `standard` tiers exclude hook-loaded extras (e.g. `packages/*/AGENTS.md` from the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook); `full` keeps everything.

## Why

The current binary split (main=all, subagent/cron=minimal) doesn't support use cases where subagents need persona context or where main sessions should be lightweight. This adds a clean, backward-compatible middle ground that lets users optimize their token budget per session type.

## How

- **`resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — resolves the effective tier from session type + optional config override.
- **`filterBootstrapFilesForSession(files, sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — extended with optional tier parameter (backward compatible — no tierOverride = existing session-type heuristic).
- **Hook-source guard** — both `minimal` and `standard` filters check `file.source !== "hook"` before allowlist matching, so a hook-loaded extra whose basename happens to be in the allowlist cannot slip in. This preserves the natural `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion order on the real hook path.
- **Config** — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` in the zod schema, TypeScript types, labels, and help text.

The `source: "root" | "hook"` provenance field on `WorkspaceBootstrapFile` is the discriminator: root-level bootstrap files keep flowing through `minimal` and `standard`; per-package extras registered via the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook are only reachable through `full`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: tiered bootstrap loading — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) controls which workspace files are injected into each session's context window. Closes #22438. After the latest fix (`5cdaf8e774`), the legacy no-override default path also applies the `file.source !== "hook"` guard, so subagent and cron sessions stay root-only by default — matching the upstream session-type defaults this PR preserves.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2 on Apple Silicon, Node 24.13.0, pnpm 11.2.2, against the fork branch tip at commit `5cdaf8e774`. A reproduction harness at the repo root (`repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`, not committed) imports the real `src/agents/workspace.ts` from this branch and invokes `resolveBootstrapTier` and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession` against an explicit `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` containing both root-loaded files and hook-loaded extras whose basenames collide with the root allowlist.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git checkout 5cdaf8e774` (this PR's head)
2. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --frozen-lockfile`
